text: How is a Dictatorship (Not) Like a Firm?
Scott Gehlbach Zhaotian Luo Spencer Pantoja

Standard organizational economics has studied the problem of a supervisor (principal) incentivizing effort from a subordinate (agent) without using perfectly enforceable contracts—instead, “relational contracts”. However, this literature has always taken for granted which player is the “principal” and which players are “agents”, something that cannot be disregarded in dictatorships. Without a sufficiently strong and interested third-party to backup the principal’s role at the rightful principal, agents have a nontrivial opportunity to usurp that position of power away from the principal. In many cases, this is their only viable outside option. If they were to quit on the dictator without challenging their power, that may be such a betrayal that leads to certain death/imprisonment. We study the problem of a dictator searching for heterogeneously competent agents and engaging in relational contracting with them, with the backstop to punish ruler deviations being an attempted coup, which with some probability leads that agent to becoming the new principal and restarting the game.
• Players. Infinite pool of players, two players active at a time.
• Timing. Infinite-horizon, discrete time t = 1 , 2 , . . . , common discount factor δ ∈ (0 , 1).

• States. The two active players are the ruler R t and the agent A t , with types θ Rt and θ At respectively. These types are public, persistent, and independently drawn: ◦ If the agent does not change A t +1 = A t , then θ A ( t +1) = θ At . ◦ If the ruler does not change R t +1 = R t , then θ R ( t +1) = θ Rt . ◦ If we have a new agent A t +1 ̸ = A t , then θ A ( t +1) ∼ F for some F with support [0 , 1]. ∗ Assume F induces a continuous probability density function. ◦ If the agent becomes the ruler R t +1 = A t , then θ R ( t +1) = θ At .

• Sequence of Play (Within Each Period) 1. R t chooses whether to fire A t . If so, period ends, and utility θ Rt accruing to R t and zero to everyone else. If not. . . 2. A t chooses an effort e t ≥ 0, at cost c ( e t ) (publicly observed), c ′ > 0. This produces output y ( θ At , e t ) which we assume. . . • is increasing in θ At (strictly when e > 0) and e t (strictly when θ A > 0) and is smooth C ∞ in both. • such that y ( θ A , e ) − c ( e t ) is quasiconcave in e for each θ A , with non-extremal peak 0 < ̄ e ( θ A ) < ∞ . • such that δy ( θ A , e ) − c ( e t ) is quasiconcave in e for each θ A and δ , with non-extremal peak 0 < ̄ e ( θ A , δ ) < ∞ . 3. R t chooses an amount of output θ Rt + y ( θ At , e t ) to share with A t , x t ∈ [0 , θ Rt + y ( θ At , e t )]. 4. A t chooses whether or not to initiate a conflict/coup. If not, the period ends and ( R t +1 , A t +1 ) = ( R t , A t ). If so, a contest occurs such that P ( R t +1 = R t ) = g ( θ Rt , θ At ) P ( R t +1 = A t ) = 1 − g ( θ Rt , θ At ) P ( A t +1 [FORMULA] A t , R t } ) = 1 assume ( θ Rt + y ( θ At , e t ) − x t , x t − c ( e t )) accrues to the ruler and agents respectively no matter the result of the conflict—current utility is consumed before the conflict begins, and the conflict only determines the next leadership. Assume ∂g ∂θ R ≤ 0 and ∂g ∂θ A ≥ 0, or that more competent agents are more likely to be successful in their coup. attempts.

• Solution Concept. An equilibrium of this game is a pair ( σ, Π) such that ◦ σ is a stationary subgame perfect Nash equilibrium strategy profile. ◦ Π: [0 , 1] → R such that Π ( θ ) is the discounted expected utility of a player of type θ becoming ruler from the perspective of before their first agent is drawn from F under strategy profile σ .

◦ We require that Π ( θ ) is the highest achievable ruler payoff for type θ given that each of their agents of type θ A anticipate a payoff of Π ( θ A ) if they were to win the conflict and become the new leader. That is, we select the ruler optimal equilibrium within a ruler’s reign , but with each ruler taking the behavior in subgames in which they are no longer the ruler as fixed and exogenuous. ∗ Essentially, normally it would be standard to take the principal optimal equilibrium in a relational contracting game. In this model, we have many possible principals, and so this is our notion of “principal optimal” that accounts for regime change. ∗ If we were to select just one of the principal’s optimal equilibrium without fixing anything, it would involve that all other agents, if they became the ruler, would not be able to sustain any relational contract i.e. repeat the static nash no-cooperation equilibrium each period. Rulers want their agents to have unprofitable outside option, and thus a ruler’s best equilibrium is for all their agents to be horrible rulers. This is not realistic because in this subgame, the original ruler is dead, so it’s not clear why everyone is still following dead ruler’s favorite equilibrium and not the current ruler’s preferred equilibrium.

1. Existence and Uniqueness of Within-Reign Ruler-Optimal Equilibria. It is not ex ante obvious that such an equilibrium would exist. For simpler versions of this model, we have proved existence and uniqueness, but still need to prove it for the general form.

2. Loyalty Competence Tradeoff with Constant g . A standard result in the literature (Egorov and Sonin, 2011) is that when the probability of a successful coup is increasing in an agent’s ability ∂g ∂θ A > 0, a ruler does not always retain the most competent agents. We show that this can hold for constant g s.t. ∂g ∂θ A = 0, due to competent agents having higher ruling outside options.

3. Incompetency an Advantage as a Ruler. Π is not monotonic in θ , the most competent rulers are not necessarily the best rulers. This is because their competency makes them more tempted to break their promises, and in this game the ruler’s temptation is what determines whether a relational contract with efficient effort provision is self-enforcing.

4. Recurrent Ruler Types. Let G ∈ [0 , 1] 2 be a directed graph such that ( θ, θ ′ ) ∈ G means that when a player of type θ is the ruler and is matched with an agent of type θ ′ , that agent is retained. For d ∈ N , let N d ( θ ) ⊆ [0 , 1] be the set of nodes reachable from θ using a directed path of at least length d . A type θ ′ is recurrent if there exists a θ such that θ ′ ∈ T i ∈ N N i ( θ ). We characterize the set of recurrent types. ◦ Interpretation. Suppose with some neglible probability (i.e. players treat it as zero), the ruler may die of natural causes, and their agent takes over. Recurrence will tell us in the long-run which rulers we would expect to see. ◦ For example, if rulers only choose agents strictly less competent than they are, the unique recurrent type is θ = 0 (idiocracy). ◦ This is recurrence from a probablistically agnostic perspective—if we are ambitious we can think about incorportating F and defining the Markov chain generated by ( F, G ). We have an uncountable state space though, which makes things more challenging measuretheoretically.
